text: Astrophysicists have long utilized chemical models to explore the complex processes within the interstellar medium (ISM) that dictate the composition of gas and dust. Recently, the predictive power of these models has shifted from qualitative to increasingly quantitative, driven by advances in experimental techniques and theoretical methods. These developments have enabled the study of individual reactions under conditions that closely mimic the ISM, providing more accurate rate constants and efficiency data. Furthermore, as chemical compositions in regions like star-forming clouds are rarely in a steady state, modern models are increasingly integrated with the dynamical evolution of physical environments. This paper assesses the current state of the art in chemical modeling for dark clouds, representing the foundational stage in the formation of stars and planetary disks.
